title: CMOS-based cryogenic control of silicon quantum circuits
type1_summary: No clearly replot-ready tabular or figure-organized data files were identified from the file inspection reports.
type2_summary: The Zenodo binary files are experiment-specific outputs consistent with raw or minimally processed benchmarking/algorithm/tomography data that likely require decoding or processing before plotting.
classification_notes: Paper evidence strongly supports the presence of raw experimental data and processed figure data in the overall repository, but the specific inspected files are all either binary experiment outputs or a documentation PDF. No clear Type 1 cleaned dataset file was evident from the file inspection reports.

file_classifications.file_evidence: PDF, 867.4 KB, from nature_source_data. This is consistent with a supplementary document or source-data PDF, not a directly plottable numeric dataset. No tabular structure or variable headers are provided in the inspection report.
file_classifications.reasoning: The file is best treated as documentation/source-data PDF rather than a Type 1 or Type 2 dataset file. It may contain figure captions, embedded plots, or explanatory material, but the inspection report does not indicate machine-readable tabular data.
file_classifications.ambiguity: Moderate: without page-level inspection, the PDF could contain source-data figures or tables, but there is no evidence it is a clean replot-ready dataset.

file_classifications.reasoning: This is most consistent with raw or minimally processed experimental data used to generate benchmarking figures. It is not obviously replot-ready from the file metadata alone and is likely to require decoding or processing.

paper_analysis.summary: This paper demonstrates a CMOS-based cryogenic control chip (“Horse Ridge”) operating at 3 K that generates tailored microwave bursts to control silicon spin qubits cooled to 20 mK. The key result is that the cryogenic controller can drive single- and two-qubit operations with fidelity comparable to room-temperature commercial instruments, enabling benchmarking protocols and a Deutsch–Jozsa algorithm on a two-qubit silicon quantum processor.
measurement_types: microwave output characterization (time-domain bursts, frequency-domain spectra)
qubit charge stability / differential current measurements
single-shot spin readout via spin-to-charge conversion
electron temperature extraction from charge-transition lines
SIMS depth profiling of isotopic/impurity concentrations

figures.description: System overview of the cryogenic quantum control setup, showing the dilution refrigerator, cryo-controller, room-temperature electronics, and the silicon quantum-dot device.
Characterization of the Horse Ridge cryogenic controller at 3 K, including its electrical performance and operating conditions.
Frequency-multiplexed qubit control using the cryo-controller, including simultaneous Rabi oscillations and fidelity benchmarks.
Programming a two-qubit silicon quantum processor with the cryo-controller, including benchmarking protocols and the Deutsch–Jozsa algorithm.
Detailed experiment setup: wiring from room-temperature AWG/VSG/MSG through filters, attenuators, switch, and cryo-controller to the quantum-dot device and readout chain.
Dilution refrigerator and packaging details for the cryo-controller chip, including enclosure, BGA package, and LO distribution hardware.
Detailed schematic of the cryo-controller digital and analog circuitry.
Power consumption breakdown and self-heating versus power for the cryo-controller.
Detailed electrical characterization of the cryo-controller output paths, including output power versus frequency, two-tone spectrum, and SNR/SFDR.
Pulse sequence used for qubit initialization, manipulation, and readout in the double quantum dot system.
Materials characterization and magnetic-field-gradient engineering: wafer stack, SIMS depth profile, micromagnet geometry, and resonance-frequency splitting mechanism.
Electron temperature measurements comparing the cryo-controller and room-temperature vector signal generator configurations.
Rabi oscillations of individual qubits driven by the cryo-controller.

paper_analysis.raw_measurement_details: The paper reports raw experimental measurements from the silicon quantum-dot device and cryo-controller, including microwave output signals, qubit readout currents (ISET), charge-stability traces, Rabi oscillation traces, benchmarking data, electron-temperature traces, power-consumption/temperature readings, and SIMS depth profiles. The text does not specify file formats or whether the repository contains unprocessed instrument files.

dataset_characterization.data_availability_statement: Data supporting this work are available at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.4061970. Code used for data acquisition and processing is from open-source Python packages QCoDeS, QTT, and PycQED.
dataset_characterization.raw_data_description: Raw measurements are implied by the experimental nature of the work, but the paper does not describe file names, formats, or exact repository organization. Likely raw modalities include digitized current traces, microwave spectra, oscilloscope/spectrum-analyzer outputs, and qubit measurement records.
dataset_characterization.processed_data_description: Processed data include extracted fidelities, fitted electron temperatures from Fermi–Dirac fits, power-consumption summaries, benchmark results, and figure-ready curves/plots. The paper explicitly mentions analysis of data presented in the main manuscript.
dataset_characterization.figure_data_description: Figure-specific data are implied to be included in the Zenodo dataset, but the paper does not explicitly state 'source data' files per figure. The figures themselves are based on experimental data and fitted/extracted quantities.
dataset_characterization.scripts_description: No custom analysis scripts or notebooks are named, but the paper states that data acquisition and processing used open-source Python packages QCoDeS, QTT, and PycQED.
dataset_characterization.notes: The dataset semantics are only partially specified. The paper clearly provides an external Zenodo archive for supporting data and names the software stack used for acquisition/processing, but it does not distinguish which files are raw versus processed, nor does it describe repository structure, metadata schema, or per-figure source-data packaging.

classification_prior.raw_data_evidence: The work is based on direct experimental measurements of cryo-controller outputs, qubit currents, oscillations, temperatures, and SIMS profiles, and the paper states that data supporting the work are available in Zenodo.
classification_prior.source_data_evidence: Main-text and extended-data figures are all derived from experimental measurements and fitted/extracted values; the paper also states that codes used for data acquisition and processing are from QCoDeS, QTT, and PycQED, implying processed figure-ready data.
classification_prior.data_availability_section_relevant: “Data supporting this work are available at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.4061970.” “The codes used for data acquisition and processing are from the open-source Python packages QCoDeS, QTT and PycQED.”

inspection_reports.note: PDF file, cannot inspect data content
